text: The surgeon performs a peritumoral injection of Indocyanine Green (ICG) into the subsorosal layer, with the fluorescence passing to the drain and leaf nodes within fifteen minutes, visualized under near-infrared light.

The surgeon employs an infracolic three-step approach to mobilize the splenic flexure using a single jockeying technique. Special camera filters are utilized to visualize the ICG fluorescence, allowing the surgeon to switch between normal and fluorescence viewing modes in high definition.

The surgeon performs a high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) using robotic hemologs. The pancreas is identified, and its tail is carefully separated from the splenic flexure of the colon.

The surgeon divides the mesentery up to the level of the small bowel and performs colonic transection. A volume of indocyanine green (ICG) is injected to visualize and check the blood supply at both ends of the bowel at the transection site using fluorescence imaging.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal anastomosis using robotic assistance, closing the enterotomy with a continuous suture. A second layer of sutures is applied in a continuous running fashion to reinforce the anastomosis.
